rescue_reasoning: Although the verbatim text only states 'Encourage adoption of efficient appliance models' (no explicit emissions token), the parent statement provides explicit mitigation framing: 'Improve energy and carbon efficiency across ... households'. Efficient appliances reduce energy demand (consistent with canonical_mechanism: efficient_appliance_adoption → energy_use_reduction), which supports Mitigation. climate_screen=explicit_parent breaks the tie in favor of inclusion.
policy_statement: Encourage adoption of efficient appliance models.
mechanism_description: Encouraging households to adopt more energy-efficient appliance models reduces residential electricity use and associated emissions from energy consumption.
primary_causal_pathway: Household appliance choice/operation → ↓ electricity demand (same service) → ↓ fossil generation → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change: Households and landlords purchase/replace appliances with higher-efficiency models and/or operate appliances with more efficient settings.  
(2) Mediating process: Higher appliance efficiency delivers the same energy services with less electricity input, reducing load on the grid and associated fuel combustion (and potentially reducing peak demand).  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower indirect (Scope 2) emissions from electricity use and, in some systems, lower marginal/peak generation emissions.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced residential electricity consumption, which lowers power-sector GHG emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: improved appliance efficiency → reduced electricity use → reduced generation emissions (GHG mitigation).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: while it can also reduce energy bills (resource efficiency co-benefit) and improve comfort, the main climate effect across all such policies is emissions reduction from lower energy demand.  
3) Edge cases applied: if the grid is near-zero carbon, the mitigation benefit shrinks but the mechanism is still primarily aimed at reducing energy use and associated emissions where applicable; rebound effects may partially offset savings but do not change the dominant intended pathway.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: any policy promoting adoption of more efficient appliances operates through reducing energy input for the same service, which (given typical grids) reduces GHG emissions—consistent across contexts.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: specific subtypes (e.g., incentives vs standards; peak-demand vs annual kWh focus) require policy-level evidence and are not assigned here.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If electricity is fully decarbonized, the primary climate benefit may shift toward resource/energy-cost efficiency rather than GHG mitigation; rebound effects and upstream embodied emissions from accelerated replacement can also affect net outcomes, but the dominant mechanism remains demand-side energy reduction.
co_benefits: Lower household energy bills; reduced peak electricity demand; improved energy security/air quality (via lower fossil generation)
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be implemented as an information campaign or an incentive program, but the text does not mention labels/awareness tools or rebates/financing; classified as planning-strategy due to generic “encourage” wording.
